explicit_text: My mother, Margaret Alice Wilson, was born in the Sand Hills of western Oklahoma shortly after the 20th century. She grew up living in a half dugout on the great plains and was a very religious woman deeply involved in the Pentecostal Holiness Church. Despite her limited formal education, she was ordained in the church and was a strong influence in our family, especially after my father died.

explicit_text: I grew up in Oklahoma, specifically around Tulsa and the western parts like Red Fork. My family lived through the Dust Bowl era, and my father worked for the Frisco railroad in Tulsa. Oklahoma shaped much of my early life, from my schooling at Daniel Webster High School to my involvement in local organizations and newspapers.

explicit_text: My father, Dwight Henry Carter, was born in Lane, Kansas, and grew up partly in Syracuse, Kansas. He was a cowboy and wrangler in his youth, riding horses and herding cattle. After the Dust Bowl, he found work with the Frisco railroad in Tulsa but tragically died in a workplace accident in December 1949. His cowboy rifle even passed down to my great-grandson, who loves to ride sheep and pretend to be a cowboy.

explicit_text: Lane, Kansas, is where my father, Dwight Henry Carter, was born. Although his family later moved around western Kansas, his mother returned to Lane because she disliked the Great Plains. My father was raised by his father in Lane before spending time in Syracuse and eventually moving west.

explicit_text: After the Dust Bowl, my father was fortunate to get a job with the Frisco railroad in Tulsa as a laborer. He painted box cars and tank cars, but tragically, in December 1949, a paint pot exploded and he was fatally burned. The Frisco railroad was a significant part of his later life and livelihood.

explicit_text: I graduated from Daniel Webster High School in Tulsa, where I was involved in school activities and even contributed to the school newspaper. Webster High was an important part of my youth and education.

explicit_text: My first newspaper job was at the West Tulsa Paper in the summer of 1950, right after I graduated from Webster High School. I worked for Kyle Tinny, who was a comedian and journalist and had bought the paper. I earned $20 a week and that experience helped launch my career in journalism.

explicit_text: After my father found work post-Dust Bowl, we lived on a small acreage in Red Fork, Oklahoma. We kept farm animals like cows, horses, pigs, and raised our own chickens and milk. It was a subsistence-type farm that gave me a pioneer-like upbringing on the prairie.

explicit_text: I attended the University of Tulsa, earning degrees in economics and later journalism in 1957 and 1959. When I started, TU was mostly a technology school aimed at preparing students for manual labor jobs, and I struggled initially without a math or foreign language background. The GI Bill helped finance my education after my active duty in the army.
